steps.title: A thermostat that runs your fan for you

narration.default: Every air conditioner, every fridge, every electric kettle has the same trick inside: a sensor watches the temperature, and the moment things cross a line, something switches on or off. Too hot? Fan kicks in. Cool enough? Fan stops. Today you'll build that exact brain. A temperature sensor will read the room, an OLED will display the number live, and an LED standing in for a fan will switch on when things heat up. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode, and the Temp & Humidity (DHT11) and U8g OLED extensions are added in Sensor Manager. If adding extensions is new to you, the Move a Servo tutorial walks you through Sensor Manager.

You've seen this block plenty of times by now. Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Setup runs once when the program starts. Loop runs forever after that, over and over, while the Arduino has power.

Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. Pin 9 is going to send voltage out to the fan — well, to an LED standing in for the fan. When pin 9 is high the fan runs; when it's low the fan stops.

steps.title: Tell the sensor where it lives

narration.default: Now drag a DHT22 attach block into setup. Set its pin to 2. This tells the sensor library which pin the temperature sensor is wired to, so when we ask for a reading later, it knows where to look. Notice we did not use pinMode this time — the DHT22 library handles all of that for us behind the scenes.

Think back to the night-lamp tutorial. The LDR returned a number from 0 to 1023 — totally raw, with no units. That number meant nothing on its own; we had to pick a threshold and guess what it stood for. But a thermostat needs degrees. Twenty-five degrees Celsius, thirty degrees Celsius — real numbers a kid in any classroom would recognise. So how on earth do we get from a raw 0-to-1023 reading to actual degrees?

steps.title: Smart sensors do the math for you

narration.default: Here's the secret. The DHT22 is not a dumb sensor like the LDR. It is a smart sensor — it has its own tiny brain inside the little case. It measures the temperature, does all the conversion maths internally, and hands your Arduino a number that is already in degrees Celsius. We don't read raw values. We don't pick scaling factors. We just ask the sensor for the temperature and it tells us. That's the whole reveal — some sensors are raw, some are smart, and the DHT22 is the smart kind.

steps.title: Pick a font for the OLED

narration.default: Drag a setFont block from the U8g OLED category into setup, below the DHT22 attach. Pick unifont — that's a clean, readable typeface. The OLED is just dots, so to draw letters it needs a font. After this line runs once at startup, every print on the OLED uses that font.

Open the Variables category. Click Make a Variable and call it temp. A variable is a labelled box you can store a number in and read back later. We need one because the temperature is going to be used twice in every loop — once to print on the OLED, and once to compare against our threshold. Reading the sensor twice would be slower and the two readings might even disagree slightly. So we read once, store in temp, and use that one value everywhere.

Drag a set variable block into the loop section. Pick temp from its dropdown. Now open the Temp and Humidity category — that's where the DHT blocks live — and drag a DHT22 read temperature block. Drop it into the value slot of the set block. Each time the loop runs, this asks the smart sensor what the temperature is right now, and parks the answer in our temp box.

Drag a firstPage block from the U8g OLED category, just under the set-temp line. The OLED draws to a hidden buffer in chunks called pages. firstPage rewinds to the top of the screen and gets ready for a fresh redraw — without it, every new reading would stack on top of the old one.

steps.title: Wrap the drawing with a picture loop

narration.default: Drag a do-while-next-page block under firstPage. This is a special C-shape — anything you drop inside its mouth gets repeated for every page chunk on the screen. firstPage starts the cycle, this picture loop walks through each chunk, and once it finishes the screen actually shows what you drew.

steps.title: Move the print position to the top

narration.default: Drag a setPrintPos block INTO the mouth of the picture loop. Set x to 0 and y to 22. That's the top-left, with y measured from the top of the letter down to its baseline — 22 leaves room for the unifont's tall characters. The next print starts right there.

Drag a print block into the picture loop, just below setPrintPos. In its value slot, build a join: the temp variable joined with the text space-C. Now every loop, the OLED writes something like twenty-five C. firstPage cleared the old reading, picture loop redraws — your display is now a live thermometer.

Time for a decision. We need to pick a threshold — a temperature where the fan switches on. In a real house, comfortable room temperature sits around twenty-two to twenty-four degrees Celsius. Anything above twenty-five starts feeling warm. So twenty-five is our line in the sand. Above twenty-five — fan on. At or below twenty-five — fan off. You can tune that number later for your own taste.

Open the Control category and drag an if-else block into the loop, below the OLED print. Same block you've used many times now. Whatever question goes in its diamond slot decides which branch runs — the if branch when the answer is yes, the else branch when the answer is no.

Open the Operators category. Drag a greater-than block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the if-else. On the left side of the greater-than, drop in the temp variable from Variables. On the right side, type 25. The question now reads: is temp greater than 25? When the room is warmer than our threshold, the answer is yes.

Drag a digitalWrite block into the if branch — the top half of the if-else. Set pin to 9 and value to high. When the answer to is-temp-greater-than-25 is yes, this line runs: pin 9 goes high, the fan switches on.

Drag another digitalWrite into the else branch — the bottom half. Pin 9, value low. When the room is at or below twenty-five, this line runs instead: pin 9 goes low, the fan stops. Sense, decide, react — same shape you've seen in night-lamp and button-led, just with degrees instead of light or button-presses.

Drag a delay block at the very bottom of the loop, after the if-else. Set it to 1000 milliseconds — one full second. Why? The temperature doesn't change quickly. Reading it ten thousand times a second is wasteful and would also flood the OLED with redraws. Once a second is plenty fast for a thermostat, and it gives the Arduino breathing room. Every loop you write should have some delay — never leave a loop running flat out. The blocks are done — let's wire up the simulator.

Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose DHT22. In the pin dialog, choose pin 2 — that's the pin we told dht22.attach about. Click Save. The virtual sensor appears next to the virtual Arduino, complete with a slider you'll use in a moment to fake a hot day.

Click the plus button again and choose SSD1306 — that's the chip name for our little OLED screen. The pin dialog will ask for SDA and SCL pins. These are the two wires the screen uses to talk to the Arduino. Set SDA to A4 and SCL to A5 — those are the standard pins on Arduino Uno for this kind of communication. Click Save. The OLED appears, blank and ready.

steps.title: Add the fan — an LED stand-in on pin 9

narration.default: Click the plus button one more time, choose LED, set pin to 9, click Save. A real fan needs a motor and a driver chip — a bit much for a beginner project. So in the simulator we use an LED to stand in for the fan: when the LED is bright, imagine the fan is spinning. When the LED is dark, the fan is off. The code is identical for a real fan once you add the motor driver.
Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino and the simulator goes live. The OLED lights up with whatever the sensor is reading right now.

The DHT22 in the simulator has a temperature slider. Drag it slowly upward. Watch the OLED — the number ticks up live. The moment the slider crosses 25 degrees, the LED turns on. Drag it back down past 25 — the LED clicks off. Sense, decide, react. The thermostat is alive.

Try this. Change the threshold from 25 to 30 — the fan kicks in only when the room gets really hot. Or add a second LED on pin 10 wired to a colder threshold like 18 — now you have a heater that switches on when the room gets cold AND a fan when it gets hot. Two thresholds, two outputs, one sensor. That's a real climate-control loop.

You just built a feedback loop — sense, decide, act, wait, repeat. The DHT22 is a smart digital sensor: it does its own measuring and hands you Celsius directly. The OLED is a buffered display: write, then push to screen with display. Threshold comparison is the same shape as the night-lamp and the plant-irrigation tutorials — sensor reads a number, code compares to a line, output reacts. That feedback-loop pattern runs every air conditioner, every fridge, every electric kettle, every greenhouse heater. You named the sensor, set the line, picked the action. The Arduino does it forever.
